title: Salesforce refresh tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Salesforce OAuth2 refresh token detected with '5Aep' prefix. Salesforce refresh tokens are long-lived OAuth2 credentials that allow applications to obtain new access tokens without requiring user re-authentication. These tokens are issued during OAuth2 authorization flows (authorization code, username-password, hybrid) and provide persistent API access to Salesforce organizations. Exposed refresh tokens enable attackers to: obtain unlimited access tokens for Salesforce REST/SOAP/Bulk/Streaming APIs, maintain persistent unauthorized access to CRM data including accounts/contacts/leads/opportunities, bypass multi-factor authentication and session timeouts, access sensitive business data and customer PII without detection, execute API calls impersonating legitimate users, modify Salesforce records and configurations, query org metadata and data structure, deploy malicious Apex code and Lightning components, exfiltrate confidential business intelligence, and maintain long-term access even after password changes. Refresh tokens remain valid until explicitly revoked and represent critical security credentials for Salesforce integrations. Immediately revoke the compromised refresh token through Salesforce Setup > Session Management or via REST API, audit all API calls associated with the token for unauthorized activity, regenerate OAuth2 credentials for the affected connected app, implement OAuth token rotation policies, use secure secrets management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault), enable IP restrictions and OAuth policies in Salesforce Setup, monitor token usage through Event Monitoring, and never commit refresh tokens to version control or expose in client-side applications.

Salesforce OAuth2 refresh token detected - generic format. Salesforce refresh tokens are long-lived OAuth2 credentials enabling applications to obtain new access tokens without user re-authentication. These tokens provide persistent API access to Salesforce CRM, allowing holders to access accounts, contacts, leads, opportunities, cases, custom objects, reports, and metadata. Exposed refresh tokens allow attackers to: generate unlimited access tokens for all Salesforce APIs (REST, SOAP, Bulk, Streaming, Metadata, Tooling), maintain persistent unauthorized access bypassing MFA and session controls, impersonate legitimate connected apps and users, access and exfiltrate sensitive customer data and business intelligence, modify critical business records and pipelines, execute SOQL/SOSL queries to discover org structure, deploy malicious Apex triggers and Lightning components, compromise single sign-on sessions, steal PII violating GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA regulations, and maintain long-term covert access as refresh tokens remain valid indefinitely until explicitly revoked. This represents a critical security vulnerability for Salesforce integrations affecting all org data and services. Immediately revoke the exposed refresh token via Salesforce Setup > Session Management > OAuth Connected Apps > Revoke Token or REST API /services/oauth2/revoke endpoint, audit Event Monitoring logs for all API calls using the compromised token, review Login History for suspicious access patterns, regenerate OAuth2 credentials for the affected connected app with reduced scopes, implement refresh token rotation policies with expiration limits, use secure secrets management with encryption at rest (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault), enable IP restrictions and OAuth policies in connected app settings, configure session timeout policies, monitor token usage through API Usage Metering, and establish incident response procedures for credential exposure.
